source.author: Nora Patel
source.body:
Overview

This playbook defines the end-to-end support lifecycle for Dedicated and Private customers (Reserved capacity, VPC/on-prem installs). It covers onboarding milestones, operational handoff criteria, support tiers and SLAs, escalation pathways, routine health checks, QBR agenda items, and common workarounds. Use this document during pre-sale handoffs, SOW signings, and the first 90 days of customer go‑live.

Scope

- Applies to Redwood customers with Dedicated capacity contracts or Private deployments (VPC or on‑prem).
- Excludes Hosted self‑serve customers; see hosted support guidelines for that flow.
- Covers technical onboarding, first‑line support (CSE/Customer Success), and engineering escalations.

Definitions

1. CSE: Customer Success Engineer (Redwood) — primary technical point of contact during onboarding and post‑go‑live.
2. TAM: Technical Account Manager — commercial/relationship owner for enterprise contracts.
3. Severity levels: P0 (service down / data loss), P1 (major degradation), P2 (partial impact), P3 (questions / minor issues).

Owner & Operating Model

- Primary owner: Customer Success team (owner_team).
- Support partners: Eng‑SRE (incidents), Eng‑Serving‑Runtime (performance/regressions), Security (sensitive incidents), Professional Services (deployment customization).
- Communication channels: Slack private channels, PagerDuty for P0/P1, Zendesk ticketing for ongoing work, JIRA for engineering bugs.

Support Tiers & SLAs

| Tier | Target response (business) | Target initial response (after hours) | Availability SLO | Typical use cases |
|------|--------------------------:|-------------------------------------:|---------------:|------------------|
| Platinum | 1 hour (P0/P1) | 2 hours | 99.95% | Global products, revenue critical |
| Enterprise (Dedicated) | 2 hours (P0/P1) | 4 hours | 99.9% | High throughput, single‑tenant pools |
| Private (on‑prem) | 4 hours (P0/P1) | 8 hours | N/A (customer infra) | Regulated / air‑gapped environments |

Notes: SLAs above reflect support response targets and availability guarantees in contracts. For Private deployments, Redwood provides support for the Redwood stack; infrastructure SLOs remain customer responsibility unless otherwise contracted.

Onboarding checklist (90‑day timeline)

1. Pre‑kickoff (sales → CS handoff)
   - Deliverables: signed SOW, network requirements, account and org IDs, requested models and capacity targets.
   - Owner: TAM + CSE
2. Week 0: Technical kickoff (within 5 business days)
   - Agenda: architecture review, network/firewall requirements, key contacts, success criteria, go‑live date.
   - Output: shared onboarding RFC and project plan.
3. Week 1–2: Environment provisioning and connectivity test
   - Tasks: allocate GPU pools (Dedicated) or validate on‑prem package install, verify routing, test TLS and KMS integration, run sample inference load test (10k tokens/min baseline).
   - Acceptance: successful end‑to‑end request/response and logs streaming to observability.
4. Week 3–4: Performance tuning & baselining
   - Tasks: collect p50/p95 latencies, throughput targets, KV cache hit rate, token cost estimates; tune batching and quantization profiles.
   - Output: Performance baseline doc and recommended runtime config (attached to ticket).
5. Week 5–8: Integration and validation
   - Tasks: implement SDK usage, validate streaming and function calling features, run privacy/security checklist (audit logging), complete sample evaluation prompts.
6. Week 9–12: Staged rollout and runbook handoff
   - Tasks: canary traffic ramp, failure injection test (see runbook steps), finalize runbooks and oncall rotation, schedule first monthly QBR.

Onboarding Handoff Criteria (must be met to declare ‘Ready for Business’)

- Successful canary serving at 10% traffic for 72 hours with no P1 regressions.
- Performance within 15% of baseline p95 targets.
- Runbook and escalation matrix published and acknowledged by customer.
- Backup and restore (model weights/kv) verified (if applicable).

Integration & Handoff Artifacts (table)

| Artifact | Owner | Where to store | Required by |
|---------|------|----------------|------------|
| Network diagram + ACL list | Customer & CSE | Confluence + secure S3 | Week 0 kickoff |
| Performance baseline report | CSE + Runtime Eng | Confluence | Week 4 |
| Runbooks (operational + failover) | CSE | Confluence | Week 9 |
| Oncall rotation & PagerDuty play | SRE | Internal oncall repo | Week 9 |

Escalation matrix & Procedures

1. P0 (Service down / data integrity risk)
   - Triage: Immediate page to SRE + CSE + TAM (use PagerDuty P0 routing).
   - First 15 minutes: Confirm scope, gather logs (request ID, node IDs, GPU pool status).
   - 15–60 minutes: Eng‑Serving‑Runtime lead and oncall SRE coordinate mitigation (rollback to previous model version / failover region).
   - Communication: Customer Slack channel + status page entry every 15 minutes.
2. P1 (Major degradation)
   - Triage: Page CSE and SRE P1 oncall.
   - Initial response within SLA target.
   - Mitigation actions: adjust batching policies, scale GPU pool, apply temporary rate limits.
3. P2/P3 (Partial impact / questions)
   - Tracked in Zendesk; CSE handles initial remediation; engineering bug filed if needed.

Contact roles and escalation contacts (example)

- Customer Success (primary): Nora Patel — CS Slack / zendesk handle @nora.p
- Technical Account Manager: Mateo Alvarez — tam@redwood.ai
- SRE oncall (P0/P1): use PagerDuty rotations (see internal rota).
- Eng‑Serving‑Runtime escalation: eng-runtime-oncall@redwood.ai

Incident Communication Templates

- Initial P0 template (post to Slack & email):
  Subject: [P0] Region X — Inference service degraded for <customer-name>
  Body: Short summary, impact (requests/sec affected), start time, immediate mitigation steps taken, next update ETA (15m).

- Postmortem structure (published within 72 hours for P0):
  1) Summary and impact numbers (duration, error count)
 2) Root cause analysis
  3) Timeline of key events
  4) Remediation & preventative actions with owners and due dates

Health Check cadence & QBR agenda

- Weekly operational health check (15–30 mins): CSE + customer SRE/TAM. Topics: error rate, p95 latency, cache hit ratio, model drift alerts, open high‑priority tickets.
- Monthly operational review: deeper metrics, billing & usage anomalies, any upcoming rollouts.
- Quarterly Business Review (QBR) template (high level):
  1) Executive summary & usage trends
  2) Reliability review (SLOs, incidents since last QBR)
  3) Performance and cost optimization recommendations (batching/quantization changes)
  4) Roadmap & model roadmap alignment
  5) Action items & owners (tracked in Confluence)

Example monitoring checks (recommended)

- p95 latency (api.generate) — warn at 200ms, critical at 300ms.
- 5m error rate (5xx) — warn at 0.5%, critical at 2%
- GPU utilization per pool — warn at 70%, critical at 85%
- KV cache hit rate — warn below 40%

YAML alert example to share with customer (for on‑prem Prometheus):

alert: HighP95Latency
expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) > 0.3
for: 5m
labels:
  severity: critical
annotations:
  summary: "p95 latency > 300ms for 5m"

Known limitations & pragmatic workarounds

- Model cold start cost: Large models may show elevated first‑token latency. Workaround: warm pools by scheduled synthetic warmup jobs.
- File‑system performance on certain on‑prem appliances: If model load times exceed 30s, stage weights onto tmpfs or use preloaded snapshot.
- Audit log gaps for early releases: If audit-log retention is shorter than required, enable S3‑forwarding and verify lifecycle policies.

Support ticket template (Zendesk)

- Subject: [P#] <short description> — <customer-org>
- Fields: severity, customer impact, request-id(s), affected model/version, region/pool, recent deploy id, attachments (logs).

Operational runbook excerpt — failover (simplified)

1) Detect: Confirm p95 latency > critical threshold + error rate spike.
2) Reduce load: Apply temporary rate limit to non‑critical routes.
3) Failover: Route traffic to standby Dedicated pool or fallback model variant.
4) Rollback: If deploy suspected, rollback to previous model in 30 minutes and monitor for stabilization.
5) Post‑incident: Start RCA and schedule fixes.

Metrics & success criteria for CSE handoff

- 99% of onboarding tasks closed in 90 days.
- No more than 1 P1 incident in first 30 days post‑go‑live.
- Performance within 15% of baseline for 95% of traffic.

Appendices

A. Reference links
- Internal PagerDuty rota: <internal-link/pagerduty/rota>
- Oncall runbook repo: <internal-link/runbooks/oncall>
- Perf baseline template: <internal-link/templates/perf-baseline>

B. Example checklist (copyable)
- [ ] Kickoff completed
- [ ] Network tests passed
- [ ] TLS & KMS verified
- [ ] E2E sample requests successful
- [ ] Performance baseline recorded
- [ ] Runbooks published
- [ ] QBR scheduled

If you have questions about applying any checklist item to a specific customer configuration, contact the CSE lead or open a Zendesk ticket with tag `dedicated-onboarding`.

- Technical Account Manager: Mateo Alvarez — tam@redwood.ai
- SRE oncall (P0/P1): use PagerDuty rotations (see internal rota).
- Eng‑Serving‑Runtime escalation: eng-runtime-oncall@redwood.ai

Incident Communication Templates

- Initial P0 template (post to Slack & email):
  Subject: [P0] Region X — Inference service degraded for <customer-name>
  Body: Short summary, impact (requests/sec affected), start time, immediate mitigation steps taken, next update ETA (15m).

- Postmortem structure (published within 72 hours for P0):
  1) Summary and impact numbers (duration, error count)
 2) Root cause analysis
  3) Timeline of key events
  4) Remediation & preventative actions with owners and due dates

Health Check cadence & QBR agenda

- Weekly operational health check (15–30 mins): CSE + customer SRE/TAM. Topics: error rate, p95 latency, cache hit ratio, model drift alerts, open high‑priority tickets.
- Monthly operational review: deeper metrics, billing & usage anomalies, any upcoming rollouts.
- Quarterly Business Review (QBR) template (high level):
  1) Executive summary & usage trends
  2) Reliability review (SLOs, incidents since last QBR)
  3) Performance and cost optimization recommendations (batching/quantization changes)
  4) Roadmap & model roadmap alignment
  5) Action items & owners (tracked in Confluence)

Example monitoring checks (recommended)

- p95 latency (api.generate) — warn at 200ms, critical at 300ms.
- 5m error rate (5xx) — warn at 0.5%, critical at 2%
- GPU utilization per pool — warn at 70%, critical at 85%
- KV cache hit rate — warn below 40%

YAML alert example to share with customer (for on‑prem Prometheus):

alert: HighP95Latency
expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) > 0.3
for: 5m
labels:
  severity: critical
annotations:
  summary: "p95 latency > 300ms for 5m"

Known limitations & pragmatic workarounds

- Model cold start cost: Large models may show elevated first‑token latency. Workaround: warm pools by scheduled synthetic warmup jobs.
- File‑system performance on certain on‑prem appliances: If model load times exceed 30s, stage weights onto tmpfs or use preloaded snapshot.
- Audit log gaps for early releases: If audit-log retention is shorter than required, enable S3‑forwarding and verify lifecycle policies.

Support ticket template (Zendesk)

- Subject: [P#] <short description> — <customer-org>
- Fields: severity, customer impact, request-id(s), affected model/version, region/pool, recent deploy id, attachments (logs).

Operational runbook excerpt — failover (simplified)

1) Detect: Confirm p95 latency > critical threshold + error rate spike.
2) Reduce load: Apply temporary rate limit to non‑critical routes.
3) Failover: Route traffic to standby Dedicated pool or fallback model variant.
4) Rollback: If deploy suspected, rollback to previous model in 30 minutes and monitor for stabilization.
5) Post‑incident: Start RCA and schedule fixes.

Metrics & success criteria for CSE handoff

- 99% of onboarding tasks closed in 90 days.
- No more than 1 P1 incident in first 30 days post‑go‑live.
- Performance within 15% of baseline for 95% of traffic.

Appendices

A. Reference links
- Internal PagerDuty rota: <internal-link/pagerduty/rota>
- Oncall runbook repo: <internal-link/runbooks/oncall>
- Perf baseline template: <internal-link/templates/perf-baseline>

B. Example checklist (copyable)
- [ ] Kickoff completed
- [ ] Network tests passed
- [ ] TLS & KMS verified
- [ ] E2E sample requests successful
- [ ] Performance baseline recorded
- [ ] Runbooks published
- [ ] QBR scheduled

If you have questions about applying any checklist item to a specific customer configuration, contact the CSE lead or open a Zendesk ticket with tag `dedicated-onboarding`.

source.title: Usage accounting: retries and streaming cancellation
source.author: Aisha Rahman
source.content:
## Overview
Some customers reconcile Redwood token usage against internal estimates and may observe higher usage totals. The most common causes are (1) client retries and (2) streaming cancellation behavior.

This page provides a support-ready explanation and recommended mitigations.

## Key definitions
- **Attempt**: a single request admitted by the Redwood API gateway (server-side).
- **Logical request**: what the customer thinks of as one user action; may map to multiple attempts if the client retries.

## What we bill
### Retries
- Redwood usage is recorded per-attempt.
- If a client retries after a timeout or non-200 response, each admitted attempt can be billable.
- The Usage dashboard lists rows by **request_id** (attempt identifier).

### Streaming cancellation
- **Prompt tokens** are counted once the request is admitted (even if the client cancels the stream later).
- **Completion tokens** are counted based on tokens actually generated/streamed prior to cancellation.

## Common customer symptoms
- We only sent N requests but we see >N rows: retries created additional attempts.
- We timed out at 15s so we expected zero bill: prompt tokens + partial completion tokens may still be counted.

## Recommended mitigations
1) Avoid automatic retries on client-side timeouts for long generations.
2) Increase client timeout where possible (align to max expected generation time).
3) Reduce max_tokens / output length to meet latency budgets.
4) For troubleshooting, ask for a small set of request_ids + the customers retry/cancel metrics.

## Support playbook (quick response blurb)
We record usage per server-admitted attempt. If your client retries after a timeout, each admitted attempt can appear separately in Usage and can be billable. For streaming, prompt tokens are counted once admitted; completion tokens reflect what was generated before cancellation.

## References
- SUP-1842 (Northwind AI escalation)
- INT-330 (UI helper text + KB link)
- Slack #support thread_ts=1739310605

source.title: Activation Signals & Churn-Mitigation Runway: Early-Warning Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.summary: Operational playbook for detecting customer activation failures and near-term churn risk, automating alerts, running deterministic remediation steps, and standardizing outreach and QBR metrics for the first 90 days and ongoing retention efforts.
source.body:
Purpose
-------
This page codifies a repeatable, measurable process to catch customers who are failing to activate or are showing early signs of churn. It combines instrumentation, automated runbook actions, human escalation, and customer-facing outreach templates so CSMs and support can react within the first 72 hours and through the 90-day handback.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all trial and self-serve customers and newly onboarded enterprise accounts (first 90 days after initial sign-up or first production API call). Excludes legacy customers with custom contracts on >6 months cadence; those follow enterprise playbooks in enterprise-onboarding.

Definitions and Key Terms
-------------------------
- Activation window: first 30 days after account creation (primary), observed through 90 days for retention patterns.
- Active API session: any authenticated request that results in a generation/embedding response (excluding health-check-only pings).
- Primary signals: a prioritized set of telemetry used to infer activation and churn risk (see Signals table).
- CSM: Customer Success Manager assigned to the account.
- MTL: Mean time to live (internal shorthand used only for SLA windows in runbooks).

Signals and Thresholds (early-warning map)
-----------------------------------------
The platform emits signal categories that are mapped to severity levels. Severity determines automated actions and human involvement. Thresholds are conservative; adjust per-customer segment (ARR bucket): SMB / Mid-Market / Enterprise.

| Signal category | Concrete metric | Severity 1 (action) | Owner | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| Activation activity | < 5 API calls in first 7 days | True | CSM + Growth | Indicates likely non-starter for PLG trials |
| Feature usage | No embedding calls after enabling embeddings feature | True | CSM | Could be integration incomplete |
| Latency regressions | 95th percentile latency > 2s above baseline for 12 hours | True | Eng oncall + Support | Potential runtime regression or region capacity issue |
| Error rate | 5xx rate > 1% sustained > 30min | True | Support + Eng oncall | Triage for infra or model variant fallback |
| Cost surprises | Daily token spend > 5x projected run-rate | True | CSM + Finance | Potential misconfiguration or abuse |
| Quality regressions | Regression alerts from eval-suite on customer prompt set | True | Applied ML + CSM | May require model rollback or prompt adjustments |

Instrumentation and Example Queries
----------------------------------
We maintain shared dashboards and alerts in Grafana/Datadog + BigQuery for retrospective signals. Below are canonical queries (adapt to dataset names).

Sample BigQuery: 7-day activation check
```
SELECT customer_id,
  COUNTIF(event_type='api_request') AS api_calls_7d,
  COUNT(DISTINCT DATE(event_ts)) AS active_days_7d
FROM `redwood-prod.telemetry.events`
WHERE customer_id = @customer_id
  AND event_ts >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
GROUP BY customer_id;
```

PromQL: 95p latency spike (Grafana alert)
```
max_over_time(histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, route))[12h:5m]) > (baseline_p95 + 2)
```

Slack alert example (format)
- channel: #cs-alerts
- message: "[ALERT] customer={{customer_id}} 95p latency spike: {{value}}ms; baseline={{baseline}}ms — CSM={{csm}}; runbook: <link>"

Automated Playbook (0–72 hour automation)
-----------------------------------------
1) Trigger: any Severity 1 signal.
2) Automated actions (system) — run within 5 minutes of alert:
   - Create a ticket in Zendesk: autotag = ew-<signal>-<customer_id>.
   - Post structured alert to #cs-alerts with runbook link and owners.
   - If signal = error-rate OR latency: automatically route traffic to fallback model variant (if available) and enable short-term caching TTL to mitigate volume.
3) System waits 30 minutes to observe whether automated mitigation reduces signal. If signal resolves, ticket annotated with automated-mitigated=true and CSM receives a summary email.
4) If signal persists after 30 minutes, escalate to human steps below.

Human Response Steps (escalation ladder)
--------------------------------------
1) First responder: CSM (primary) initiates customer outreach and opens a triage call if customer is impacted. Use the "Initial check-in" email template below. Within 60 minutes: confirm receipt in Zendesk and update Slack thread.
2) If impact = platform (latency/error): Support (Tier 2) owns technical triage; engage Eng oncall if degradation continues > 30 minutes.
3) If impact = integration or misconfiguration: CSM/Solutions Eng run guided troubleshooting (see Integration checklist).
4) If customer financial exposure > $5k/day or SLA breach likely: Immediately escalate to Customer Success Lead and Sales Engineering for executive comms.

Escalation Matrix (contact window and timeout)
---------------------------------------------
| Escalation step | Role | Contact method | Timeout |
|---|---|---|---:|
| 1 | CSM | Slack + email | 60 min |
| 2 | Support Tier 2 | Pager/Slack | 30 min |
| 3 | Eng oncall | Pager + call bridge | 15 min |
| 4 | CS Lead / Sales Eng | Phone + executive email | 60 min |
| 5 | Legal / Finance (if billing risk) | Email + Ops channel | 4 hours |

Customer Outreach Templates (use and track)
------------------------------------------
Initial check-in (short—sent after automated mitigation or first responder acknowledges):
Subject: Quick check — we saw low activity / a problem on your Redwood account
Hi {{first_name}},

Wanted to check in: our systems detected low activity / an alert for your account ({{customer_id}}) that may affect your initial setup. We ran an automated mitigation and wanted to confirm whether you see degraded responses or errors. Can you reply with: (1) whether you attempted to run a request in the last hour, and (2) any error messages you see?

If you prefer, we can jump on a 15-minute troubleshooting call — here is a link to book: {{calendar_link}}

Thanks,
{{csm_name}}

Post-resolution (explain what happened + next steps):
Subject: Incident summary and next steps — {{incident_id}}\Hi {{first_name}},

Thanks for your patience. We identified {{root_cause}} and took {{actions}} to remediate. To prevent recurrence we will: 
- Action 1: {{permanent_fix}} (owner: ENG)
- Action 2: {{guidance}} (owner: CSM)

We recommend the following product changes for your integration: {{recommendations}}.

If you’d like, we can include this item on your QBR agenda so your team has visibility.

Guided troubleshooting checklist (for CSMs / Solutions Eng)
1) Confirm API key scopes and region configuration.
2) Check ingestion of customer config flags and feature toggles.
3) Reproduce the failure with a minimal request (include request/response examples).
4) If errors are 5xx, capture request_id and escalate to Support with logs and timing.
5) For perceived low-quality outputs, run the customer prompt set through the eval harness and attach results.

30/60/90-Day Activation & Retention Playbook (CSM cadence)
---------------------------------------------------------
- Day 0–7: Activate
  - Tasks: Welcome call, verify API keys, run reference sample requests, ensure first successful generation in sandbox.
  - KPI guardrails: >= 5 successful API calls and >= 3 active days in first 7 days.
- Day 8–30: Adopt
  - Tasks: Assist with embedding/indexing flow or fine-tune integration; ship 1 internal example that uses production tokens.
  - KPI guardrails: token usage >= projected onboarding plan; at least one quality check using eval harness.
- Day 31–90: Expand
  - Tasks: QBR prep, map success metrics, recommend Dedicated/Private options if predictable throughput needed.
  - KPI guardrails: 30-day retention > 60% for target segment; expansion activity (feature upsell or reserved capacity ask) reviewed by Sales.

QBR Metrics & Dashboard (standardized deck)
------------------------------------------
Include these sections in every QBR slide set for churn-risk customers and for accounts at renewal:
- Executive summary: status (Green/Amber/Red), ARR, product usage trends.
- Adoption metrics: DAU/WAU, avg tokens/day, top API routes used.
- Reliability metrics: uptime, error rate, p95 latency, incidents since last QBR.
- Cost & efficiency: cost per 1k tokens, unusual spend patterns.
- Quality: eval harness results on customer prompt set, customer-reported quality issues.
- Action plan: next 90-day objectives, owners, and measurable targets.

Retention Experiments & Hypotheses (R&D adjacent)
------------------------------------------------
We recommend small, measurable experiments to reduce early churn. Example hypotheses and experiment designs: 
- Hypothesis: A 15-minute onboarding call within the first 48 hours increases 30-day activation by 25%. Experiment: randomized control group on new signups for 8 weeks.
- Hypothesis: Automated onboarding nudges (sample code snippets tailored to detected SDK language) reduce time-to-first-success by 40%. Experiment: A/B test within trial bucket.

Postmortem & Continuous Improvement
----------------------------------
- After any Severity 1 event affecting an active customer, complete a 1-pager postmortem: timeline, root cause, fixes, preventative actions, and owner for each action. Attach to the Zendesk ticket and QA during the weekly CS Ops review.
- Quarterly: review activation thresholds by segment; update detection logic if false positive rate > 10% for a given signal.

Ownership and Review Cadence
---------------------------
- Playbook owner: Head of Customer Success (Priya Natarajan).
- Reviewers (operational): Support Lead (Lucas Chen), Applied ML (Maya Patel), Platform SRE (Elena Rodriguez).
- Review cadence: monthly for the first 6 months after rollout, then quarterly.

Appendix A — Known integration gotchas (quick references)
-------------------------------------------------------
- Region mismatch: customer keys scoped to eu-west but calls hitting us-east leading to silent failures. Check key region claim in token metadata.
- Unbounded streaming: customers who forget to set max_tokens or streaming chunk size can incur runaway spend; recommend SDK defaults in customer onboarding templates.
- Cache TTL pitfalls: setting prefix cache TTL too long may mask upstream model regressions; prefer short TTLs during active troubleshooting.

Appendix B — Links and runbook artifacts
----------------------------------------
- Shared dashboards: Grafana:LinkToActivationDashboards (internal)
- Runbooks: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/incident-triage-latency.json
- Ticket template: Support Ticket Template (Zendesk) link

Change log
----------
- 2024-10-03: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2024-12-14: Added automated mitigation steps and Slack templates (reviewer: Lucas Chen).
- 2025-02-12: Updated thresholds for latency and error-rate; added QBR metric standardization (reviewer: Maya Patel).
